channel.title: My notes
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Bright Star
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Bright Star.md

item.title: John Keats
item.description:
under the guardianship of two London merchants appointed by his maternal grandmother after his mother’s death. withdrawn from the Clarke’s School at age of 15 by his guardian to apprentice with an apothecary-surgeon and study medicine in a London hospital. became a licensed apothecary(药剂师) in 1816, but never practiced this profession, deciding instead to write poetry. Met Leigh Hunt around 1816, an influential editor of the Examiner, who published his sonnets "On First Looking into Chapman's Homer" and "O Solitude." Published his first volume, Poems by John Keats, in 1817.(Shelley fond Keats and advised him to polish his poem but Keats didn't follow) Published Endymion, a four-thousand-line erotic/allegorical romance in 1818. contracted tuberculosis in the autumn of 1819. published his third and best volume of poetry, Lamia, Isabella, The Eve of St. Agnes, and Other Poems in July 1820, which also contains the unfinished Hyperion, and three poems considered among the finest in the English language: Ode on a Grecian Urn, Ode on Melancholy, and Ode to Nightingale. died in Rome on February 23, 1821, at the age of twenty-five, and was buried in the Protestant cemetery last request: to be buried under an unnamed tombstone, with words "Here lies one whose name was written in water" a theory developed by Keats the ability to exist in the condition of uncertainties, miseries, doubts etc searching for possibility that by uniting Truth and Beauty in a single sublime experience. Art possesses the power to overcome the world of pain and death, and to redeem man’s doubts and uncertainties through a brief spiritual transcendence. A famous theory of poetry What the imagination seizes as Beauty must be truth The message conveyed in his poetry therefore is the lasting power of beauty and its union with truth a meditation on the immortal beauty of the nightingale's song and the sadness of the poet who actually responds to the beauty of the bird's song with both happiness and ache. Ode on a Grecian Urn (5) O Attic shape! Fair attitude! with brede Of marble men and maidens overwrought, With forest branches and the trodden weed; Thou, silent form, dost tease us out of thought As doth eternity: Cold Pastoral! When old age shall this generation waste, Thou shalt remain, in midst of other woe Than ours, a friend to man, to whom thou say'st, "Beauty is truth, truth beauty,"—that is all Ye know on earth, and all ye need to know. The Greek power of art lie in capturing beauty of nature and On First Looking into Chapman's Homer
Bright Star
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/John Keats.md

item.title: On First Looking into Chapman's Homer
item.description: Why there is no any line or words of Chapman's translation: Keats want to show his surprise and excitement of discovering new things rather than to make a comparison or focus on detial
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/On First Looking into Chapman's Homer.md

item.title: 英美诗歌与文化
item.description:
Poetry is spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings; it takes its origin from emtion recollected in tranquility --- William Wordworth A living poem begins with a lump in the throat a homesickeness or a lovesickness. It is a reaching out toward expression, an effort to find fulfillment. A complete poem is one where emtion has found its thought and thought has found the words. ---Robert FrostPoetry delights and instructs. Poetry communicates universal experience. Iambic foot/ Iamb a unit of unstressed and stressed syllables that used in sentece Iambic pentameter which is a form of poetic meter consisting of five pairs of alternating unstressed and stressed syllables. (the red can changed into other types of -meter eg. trimeter) his works are not temporate but immortal, its' contains something that is universal and we can still learn something from it. sonnet 19 by Milton -- story of talent -- story of day-labour
sonnet 1 by Milton
item.link: 诗歌文化/英美诗歌与文化.html
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/英美诗歌与文化.md

item.title: sonnet 19 by Milton
item.description: John MiltonWhen I consider how my light is spent, Ere half my days in this dark world and wide, And that one talent which is death to hide Lodged with me useless, though my soul more bentTo serve therewith my Maker, and present My true account, lest He returning chide, 'Doth God exact day-labour, light denied?' I fondly ask. But Patience, to preventThat murmur, soon replies: 'God doth not need Either man's work or his own gifts. Who best Bear his mild yoke, they serve him best. His stateIs kingly: thousands at his bidding speed, And post o'er land and ocean without rest; They also serve who only stand and wait.' On His Blindness - Rearranged ( the first 8 lines): I fondly ask, "Doth God exact day-labor, light denied?" when I consider (1) how my light is spent ere half my days in this dark world and wide and (2) (how) that one talent which is death to hide lodged with me useless(ly) though my soul (be) more bent to serve therewith my Maker, and (to) present my true account, lest He ( i.e. my maker) returning (should) chide (me)...# On His Blindness - Rearranged ( the first 8 lines): I fondly ask, "Doth God exact day-labor, light denied?" when I consider (1) how my light is spent ere half my days in this dark world and wide and (2) (how) that one talent which is death to hide lodged with me useless(ly) though my soul (be) more bent to serve therewith my Maker, and (to) present my true account, lest He ( i.e. my maker) returning (should) chide (me)... Bear his mild yoke, they serve him best. His state Is kingly: thousands at his bidding speed, And post o'er land and ocean without rest; They also serve who only stand and wait.' => you can serve god more than one way => so although Milton is blind, he still believe god and will continue to serve the god (physically can't but mentally can) We can learn from this poem: regardless your output, pay attention to your attitude, you should be active and do things God requires service. The poet is blind, but the poem does not deal with his physical handicap, but his spiritual one. The solutions that the poem offers are spiritual.
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/sonnet 19 by Milton.md

item.title: Composed by the Sea-Side near Calais
item.description: William Wordsworth Fair Star of evening, Splendour of the west,Star of my Country!--on the horizon’s brinkThou hangest, stooping, as might seem, to sinkOn England’s bosom; yet well pleased to rest,Meanwhile, and be to her a glorious crestConspicuous to the Nations. Thou, I think,Should’st be my Country’s emblem; and should’st wink,Bright Star! with laughter on her banners, drestIn thy fresh beauty. There! that dusky spotBeneath thee, that is England; there she lies.Blessings be on you both! one hope, one lot,One life, one glory!--I, with many a fearFor my dear Country, many heartfelt sighs,Among men who do not love her, linger here.it's a Sonnet, Petrarchan sonnet The Peace of Amiens was a treaty signed on March 25,1802 between France and Britain; but this peace are temporary, soon Britain started the war again.
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Composed by the Sea-Side near Calais.md

item.title: I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud
item.description: a lyric poem focusing on the poet's response to the beauty of nature four six-line stanzas following a quatrain-couplet rhyme scheme: ababcc. with each line metered in iambic tetrameter Wordsworth, his sister and his wife were come together to see the daffodilsWilliam WordsworthI wandered lonely as a Cloud That floats on high o'er Vales and Hills, When all at once I saw a crowd, A host of golden Daffodils; Beside the Lake, beneath the trees, Fluttering and dancing in the breeze.Continuous as the stars that shine And twinkle on the Milky Way, They stretched in never-ending line Along the margin of a bay: Ten thousand saw I at a glance, Tossing their heads in sprightly dance.The waves beside them danced, but they Out-did the sparkling waves in glee: A Poet could not but be gay In such a jocund company: I gazed—and gazed—but little thought What wealth the shew to me had brought:For oft when on my couch I lie In vacant or in pensive mood, They flash upon that inward eye Which is the bliss of solitude, And then my heart with pleasure fills, And dances with the Daffodils
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud.md

item.title: Shakespeare and his works
item.description:
William ShakespeareBorn in startford-upon-Avon,1564.4.26 learning in gramma school married early in his 18 | a tiger's heart warpt in player's(woman's原句) body published his first two long poems have his own theatre get a Coat of Arms(家徽)an experimental period 1591-1593 Titus Andronicus; Love's Labour's Lost; The Taming of Shrew; The Comdy Of Errors the second period 1594-1601 Romeo and Juliet; the Merchant of Venice; A Midsummer-Night's Dream; Much Ado about nothing; The Merry Wives Of Windsor; Juilius Ceaser the third period 1602-1610 Hamlet; Macbeth; Othello; King Lear; Twelfth Night; All's Well that Ends Well; Antony and Cleopatra the final period 1611 Cymbeline; Henry VIII; The TempestShakespeare's works publishcation
Shakespeare's works' theme
sonnet 18
sonnet 19
Sonnet 43
sonnet 101
sonnet 116dark lady sonnets
sonnet 127
sonnet 130
sonnet 133
sonnet 86
The Phoenix and Turtle (Turtle Dove) |
The Canonization BY JOHN DONNE
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/Shakespeare and his works.md

item.title: William Wordsworth
item.description:
Poetry is spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings; it takes its origin from emotion recollected in tranquility. Poetry is the first and last of all knowledge — it is as immortal as the heart of man. —William Wordsworthone of the greatest poet Romantic poet English Romantic MovementBorn in 1770 in the Lake District of northern England. And received a good education. lost his mother when he was 7 and lost his father at 13 sent off by guardian uncles to grammar school at Hawkshead, moved on in 1787 to St. John's College, Cambridge. spend long time in France, and met a women who is one of his lover. They have a daughter but because of war, Wordsworth had to go back England in 1789 and didn't come back France until 1802 although he already have a daughter with the Frechen women but he married another English women
Composed by the Sea-Side near Calais
I Wandered Lonely as a Cloud
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/William Wordsworth.md

item.title: Tragedy
item.description: "A very sad event or situation, especially one that involves death." — Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary "The term is broadly applied to literary, and especially to dramatic, representations of serious actions which eventuate in a disastrous conclusion for the protagonist (the chief character)." — M.H. Abrams, A Glossary of Literary Terms"Tragedy, then, is an imitation of an action that is serious, complete, and of a certain magnitude; in language embellished with each kind of artistic ornament, the several kinds being found in separate parts of the play; in the form of action, not of narrative; through pity and fear effecting the proper purgation of these emotions." —Aristotle, Poetics "A change not to prosperity from adversity, but on the contrary from prosperity to adversity, caused not by depravity but by a great error (hamartia) of a character". —Aristotle, Poetics "The plot should be so structured that, even without seeing it performed, the person who hears the events that occurs experiences horror and pity at what comes about." —Aristotle, PoeticsOrigin of Tragedy City Dionysia Festival Dionysus, God of WineAncient Greek Tragedians Aeschylus : Prometheus Bound Sophocles: Oedipus the King (become motif of pass generations tragedy) Euripides: Medeathe Shakespeare's Tragedy To use Aristotle way to analyze Shakespeare's works is like to judge one's action by another country's laws because the long middle age, the ancient Greek's work is unfamiliar to people until RenaissanceSo in the times of Shakespeare, the source of Tragedy is from Rome. Classical influence Seneca’s Roman Plays ​Content: Violent and sensational themes. ​Style: Development of an elevated rhetoric. ​Key Example: The Spanish Tragedy by Thomas Kyd ​Features: Ghost, insanity, suicide, sensational incidents, a play-within-a-play, and a bloody ending. ​Fall of Princes or an Individual Tradition ​(Note: This section is the beginning of a new topic in the lecture slide regarding the transition of tragic traditions.) Native Tradition mystery cycle plays medieval morality plays combine high and low "But besides these gross absurdities, how all their plays be neither right tragedies, nor right comedies, mingling kings and clowns, not because the matter so carrieth it, but thrust in the clown by head and shoulders, to play a part in majestical matters, with neither decency nor discretion, so as neither the admiration and commiseration, nor the right sportfulness, is by their mongrel tragi-comedy obtained.” —Sir Philip Sidney, Apology for Poetry
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/Tragedy.md

item.title: English Romantic Movement
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/English Romantic Movement.md

item.title: sonnet 1 by Milton
item.description: John MiltonO nightingale, that on yon bloomy spray Warbl'st at eve, when all the woods are still, Thou with fresh hope the lover's heart dost fill, While the jolly hours lead on propitious May,Thy liquid notes that close the eye of Day, First heard before the shallow cuckoo's bill Portend success in love; O if Jove's will Have linkt that amorous power to thy soft lay,Now timely sing, ere the rude Bird of Hate Foretell my hopeless doom, in some Grove nigh: As thou from year to year hast sung too late For my relief; yet hadst no reason why,Whether the Muse, or Love call thee his mate, Both them I serve, and of their train am I.
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/sonnet 1 by Milton.md

item.title: cuckoo in western culture
item.description: betray, cheat in love because of cuckoo is regraded as betray
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/cuckoo in western culture.md

item.title: The Canonization BY JOHN DONNE
item.description: Canonization : 成圣，封为圣人 For God's sake hold your tongue, and let me love, Or chide my palsy, or my gout,chide : 指责，斥责 palsy : 瘫痪 gout : 痛风 My five gray hairs, or ruined fortune flout,With wealth your state, your mind with arts improve,art : knowledge Take you a course, get you a place,Observe his honor, or his grace,Or the king's real, or his stampèd faceto comtemplate the king's real or his stampèd face stampèd: 印，stamp Contemplate; what you will, approve,So you will let me love.the author didn't care the success mentioned above, he only want to love Alas, alas, who's injured by my love?What merchant's ships have my sighs drowned?Who says my tears have overflowed his ground?When did my colds a forward spring remove?When did the heats which my veins fillAdd one more to the plaguy bill?plaguy bill: 瘟疫名单，记录死于瘟疫的人都名单 Soldiers find wars, and lawyers find out stillLitigious men, which quarrels move,总有战争，总有纠纷（诉讼） Though she and I do love.上面都是很典型的[[Petrarchan Conceit]] 使用反问的方式,展示love // Petrarchan Conceit Call us what you will, we are made such by love;Call her one, me another fly,We're tapers too, and at our own cost die,fly : 飞蛾 taper : 蜡烛 And we in us find the eagle and the dove.The phœnix riddle hath more witBy us; we two being one, are it.So, to one neutral thing both sexes fit.We die and rise the same, and proveMysterious by this love.We can die by it, if not live by love,And if unfit for tombs and hearseOur legend be, it will be fit for verse;tombs, hearse : for the king or high lord but we can in verse And if no piece of chronicle we prove,We'll build in sonnets pretty rooms;chronicle : also for high lords sonnet As well a well-wrought urn becomesThe greatest ashes, as half-acre tombs,And by these hymns, all shall approveUs canonized for Love.点题 And thus invoke us: "You, whom reverend loveinvoke : pray for reverend : respected Made one another's hermitage;hermitage: 隐居 You, to whom love was peace, that now is rage;Who did the whole world's soul contract, and droveInto the glasses of your eyescontract : 缩小 (So made such mirrors, and such spies,That they did all to you epitomize)Countries, towns, courts: beg from aboveA pattern of your love!"
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/The Canonization BY JOHN DONNE.md

item.title: The Phoenix and Turtle (Turtle Dove)
item.description: First published in London: 1601 Title Loves MArtyr: OR, Rosalins Complaint. theme: what is love what is true lovemeter:3 (precisely: 3.5)in this poem there are some many paradoxes'A paradox is a statement which seems on its face to be logically contradictory or absurd, yet turns out to be interpretable in a way that makes good sense.' ---- M. H. Abrams, A Glossary of Literary Terms"The language of poetry is the language of paradox. Paradox is the language of sophistry, hard, bright, witty; it is hardly the language of the soul." ---- Cleanth Brooks, The Well Wrought Urn => Shakespeare decided to use paradox to write this poem, because he want to innovate his expression about enteral theme--lovelove in that time is equal to goodness so the urn now contains true, love(goodness), beauty --sonnet 115"The Phoenix and turtle" is the transitional piece that symbolically links ancient, mediaeval, Elizabethan and metaphysical modes of expression and thought. Fusing Sydney's golden age smoothness with the kind of dense scholastic argumentation we encounter in Donne.James Bednarz《凤凰与斑鸠》是一部过渡性作品，它在象征层面上连接了古代、中世纪、伊丽莎白时代与玄学派的表达方式与思想。它融合了西德尼黄金时代的流畅文风，以及我们在多恩作品中所见的那种密集的经院式论证。—— 詹姆斯・贝德纳茨
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/The Phoenix and Turtle (Turtle Dove).md

item.title: sonnet 115
item.description: Fair, kind, and true, is all my argument,Fair, kind, and true, varying to other words;And in this change is my invention spent,Three themes in one, which wondrous scope affords.Fair, kind, and true, have often lived alone,Which three till now, never kept seat in one.But all together they make up the sum,And all together they do me become.O, how much more doth beauty beauteous seemBy that sweet ornament which truth doth give!The rose looks fair, but fairer we it deemFor that sweet odour which doth in it live.The canker blooms have full as deep a dyeAs the perfumed tincture of the roses,Hang on such thorns and play as wantonlyWhen summer's breath their masked buds discloses:But, for their virtue only is their show,They live unwooed and unrespected fade,Die to themselves. Sweet roses do not so;Of their sweet deaths are sweetest odours made:And so of you, beauteous and lovely youth,When that shall fade, my verse distills your truth.
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/sonnet 115.md

item.title: sonnet 130
item.description: Back that time all poem for love or pretty is misrepresented whatever the real appearance of the girl is, the poem always the same "white, black hair, red lip etc" so called deceitful beauty. So this poem is a innovation and an irony for the other poemsconceit or Petrarchan ConceitOriginally meaning a concept or image, ‘conceit’ came to be the term for figures of speech which establish a striking parallel, usually ingeniously elaborate, between two very dissimilar things or situations. examples I find no peace; and all my war is done; I fear and hope; I burn and freeze in ice. (Sir Wyatt)
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/sonnet 130.md

item.title: Petrarchan Conceit
item.description: Originally meaning a concept or image, ‘conceit’ came to be the term for figures of speech which establish a striking parallel, usually ingeniously elaborate, between two very dissimilar things or situations. examples I find no peace; and all my war is done; I fear and hope; I burn and freeze in ice. (Sir Wyatt)
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/Petrarchan Conceit.md

item.title: Nightingale in western culture
item.description: good luck, successful in love
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Nightingale in western culture.md

item.title: Synesthetic Metaphor
item.description: Synesthetic metaphor is a figure of speech in which a word or phrase that normally describes one sensory modality (e.g., sight, hearing, touch, taste, smell) is used to describe another, different sensory modality.通感隐喻是一种修辞手法，指将通常用于描述某一种感官范畴（如视觉、听觉、触觉、味觉、嗅觉）的词语或短语，用来描述另一种不同的感官范畴。
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Synesthetic Metaphor.md

item.title: Apostrophe
item.description: Apostrophe is a figure of speech in which a speaker directly addresses an absent person, a deceased individual, an abstract concept, or an inanimate object as if it were present, alive, and capable of responding. 呼告是一种修辞手法，指说话者直接对不在场的人、已故者、抽象概念或无生命的物体进行称呼，仿佛对方就在眼前、具有生命且能够回应。
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Apostrophe.md

item.title: story of day-labour
item.description: 1 For the kingdom of heaven is like a landowner who went out early in the morning to hire workers for his vineyard.2 He agreed to pay them a denarius for the day and sent them into his vineyard.3 About nine in the morning he went out and saw others standing in the marketplace doing nothing.4 He told them, “You also go and work in my vineyard, and I will pay you whatever is right.”5 So they went. He went out again about noon and about three in the afternoon and did the same thing.6 About five in the afternoon he went out and found still others standing around. He asked them, “Why have you been standing here all day long doing nothing?”7 “Because no one has hired us,” they answered. He said to them, “You also go and work in my vineyard.”8 When evening came, the owner of the vineyard said to his foreman, “Call the workers and pay them their wages, beginning with the last ones hired and going on to the first.”9 The workers who were hired at about five in the afternoon came and each received a denarius.10 So when those came who were hired first, they expected to receive more. But each one of them also received a denarius.11 When they received it, they began to grumble against the landowner.12 “These who were hired last worked only one hour,” they said, “and you have made them equal to us who have borne the burden of the work and the heat of the day.”13 But he answered one of them, “I am not being unfair to you, friend. Didn’t you agree to work for a denarius?14 Take your pay and go. I want to give the one who was hired last the same as I gave you.15 Don’t I have the right to do what I want with my own money? Or are you envious because I am generous?”16 So the last will be first, and the first will be last.theme: God don't care your quantity of work but your actions, as long as you acted, you will gain the praisedenarius : amount of moneyrecorded in Gospel of Matthew 20
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/story of day-labour.md

item.title: story of talent
item.description: back the ancient time, talent used as a measurement of money and the Lord give his servants talents according to their abilities then the Lord went out to journey and the one who have five talents gain or earn another five talents the one who gained two talents do the same thing but the one who only got a talent who just dig a hole and hide his talentwhen the Lord came back the one got five talents give back the five talents and the five he earned. Then God praised him good and faithful (because of his diligent and obey god's will) and can enter the joy of thy lord. the one got two talents also give back all four talents, and god praised him in the same way but the one who had one talent said the god is powerful does not need his servants do anything. The Lord can do anything. So he hid his talent. God blame hime wicked and slothful and should be thrown into outer darknesstheme and message => We should take care what we be gifted or given(keep it well). We should use the gifts to create more. We have responsibility to use the gifts(to do the second thing).talents : resource, talent etc. servants : the common peoplerecorded in Gospel of Matthew 25
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/story of talent.md

item.title: Four Gospels
item.description: Gospels of Matthew the first Gospels and the longest Mark Luke John

item.title: Sonnet
item.description: a fixed foerm of poetry with its own rhyme scheme(Iambic Pentameter <-> Trochee扬抑格) composed of fourteen linesmost of this written in iambic pentametertypes of sonnets Italian / Petrarchan sonet first stanza called Octave that compsed of frst two Quantrains which is abba abba and a Sestet(another stanza) follwoed which is cdcdcd or cdecde etc. some famous Italian sonneteer : Dante Alighieri(但丁), Francesco Petrarch, Michelangelo(米开朗基罗) rhyme scheme: abba abba cde cde / cd cd cd English / Shakespearean sonnet three quatrains and a couplet in most cast, it's meter is iambic pentameter rhyme scheme abab abab efef gg English / Spenserian sonnet also during renaissance times rhyme scheme abab bcbc cdcd ee Sir Thomas Wyatt Spencer Millton William Wordsworth John Keats
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Sonnet.md

item.title: John Milton
item.description:
One of the greatest and most influential poets, ranking with Chaucer and Shakespeare. writing both poetry and prose best know works Paradise Lost known as "the lady of Christ's" because of Christ's College, Cambrige University who wrote 23 sonnets and 1-6 and 23 about love; 15-18 about public policy; 8,11,12 about peace and freedom; 9,10, 14 addressing female friends and 13,20,21 addressing male friends, 7,19,22 about his will to serve God. but Milton is a quite religious person so every his works contains religious belief
Blank verseMiltonic sonnetKeeps the Petrarchan length and rhyming scheme but don't have the stanza break between the octave and the sestet
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/John Milton.md

item.title: sonnet 18
item.description: Shall I compare thee to a summer’s day? (similie)Thou art more lovely and more temperate: Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May, (personification: summer->tenant)And summer’s lease hath all too short a date; Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines, (metaphor: the eye of heaven->sun)And often is his gold complexion dimm'd; (complextion: 面色，脸色) (personification: his gold complextion dimm'd->sunshine is too weak)And every fair from fair sometime declines, (alliteration: fair from fair; chance or nature's changing course) (the first fair refers to beauty concept itself and the second fair refers to person)By chance or nature’s changing course untrimm'd; (untrimmed未加修饰的)But thy eternal summer shall not fade, (metaphor: eternal summer -> eternal beauty) Nor lose possession of that fair thou ow’st; Nor shall death brag thou wander’st in his shade, (brag: 吹嘘)When in eternal lines to time thou grow’st: keep your beauty in peom will be as eternal as time the theme is language, poem will be eternal So long as men can breathe or eyes can see,    So long lives this, and this gives life to thee. So long as -> as long as So long live this -> so this will live eternal收录于1609年出版的《莎士比亚十四行诗》，共154首；本诗属Fair Youth系列（1–126首），献给一位神秘贵族青年结构：3个四行联（quatrains）+ 1个双行对句（couplet），共14行 韵律：ABAB CDCD EFEF GG 节奏：抑扬格五音步 Iambic pentameterThe four steps (structure) of Shakespeare sonnet Introduction (opening) Elucidation of the theme (developing; raising problems) Transition to another viewpoint (changing) Summing uo (concluding and may reinforce the theme; give a solution) 类似中国的 起承转合 Because of geography, in Britain spring still really cold, but summer is mild, so compare some on to summer in Britain is the highest praise Shakespeare even elevate it higher, Thou art more lovely and more temperate; summer is good but still transient but you are better and eternal Introduction (opening) you are so beautiful Elucidation of the theme (developing; raising problems) but every beautiful things will fade eventually Transition to another viewpoint (changing) but your beauty will not fade Summing uo (concluding and may reinforce the theme; give a solution) because language and poem will eternal exist and you will be in this poem the same theme poems sonnet55 sonnet60
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/sonnet 18.md

item.title: Sonnet 43
item.description: Michelangelo created this kind of "night and day" comparison
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/Sonnet 43.md

item.title: sonnet 127
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/sonnet 127.md

item.title: sonnet 116
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/sonnet 116.md

item.title: sonnet 101
item.description: O truant Muse, what shall be thy amendsFor thy neglect of truth in beauty dyed?Both truth and beauty on my love depends;So dost thou too, and therein dignified.Make answer, Muse: wilt thou not haply say, (haply:happly or don't have any means)“Truth needs no colour, with his colour fix’d;Beauty no pencil, beauty’s truth to lay;But best is best, if never intermix’d”?Because he needs no praise, wilt thou be dumb?Excuse not silence so, for’t lies in theeTo make him much outlive a gilded tomb (gilded tomb: the frequently appeared image:immortal,enteral)And to be praised of ages yet to be.Then do thy office, Muse; I teach thee howTo make him seem long hence as he shows now.(write into poem)So we can concluded that Muse here is a symbol of inspiration, the author blame the inspiration did come怠惰的诗神啊，你该如何补偿你竟轻慢了融于美中的真纯？真与美皆栖于我爱人的身上；你也依托于他，才得这份尊荣。回答吧，诗神！你莫非会辩称：“真本有定色，何须彩墨来装点；美自有真容，何须画笔来勾描；至善本天成，不待混染方显绝？”难道因他无需称颂，你就缄口不言？别用这借口搪塞，你的天职本就是让他超越鎏金的陵寝，寿比苍天让未竟的世代，都将他称颂不止。尽你的本分吧，诗神！我来教你如何让他此刻的风华，永存于万世。
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/sonnet 101.md

item.title: Miltonic sonnet
item.description: Keeps the Petrarchan length and rhyming scheme but don't have the stanza break between the octave and the sestet
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Miltonic sonnet.md

item.title: Blank verse
item.description: features un-rhymed no fixed of lines types Iamb pentameter blank verse Trochee Anapest Dactyl there is no and never will have stress stress syllable together
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Blank verse.md

item.title: Christ's College, Cambrige University
item.description: Famous students John Milton Charles Darwinfirst established as Gods's house in 1437 by William Byngham the British college have kind of independency, have more than on discipline（学科）
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Christ's College, Cambrige University.md

item.title: sonnet60
item.description: Like as the waves make towards the pebbled shore, (simile) So do our minutes hasten to their end; (personify); \ \one life end and another life beginEach changing place with that which goes before, In sequent toil all forwards do contend. sequent toil: continuous, laborious effortNativity, once in the main of light, nativity: a newborn or the state of being bornCrawls to maturity, wherewith being crown'd, Crooked eclipses 'gainst his glory fight, And Time that gave doth now his gift confound. crowned and crooked all means become you climb up and the go down =>time gives and time take it awayTime doth transfix the flourish set on youth And delves the parallels in beauty's brow, Feeds on the rarities of nature's truth, And nothing stands but for his scythe to mow: time gradually but steadily devour everythingAnd yet to times in hope, my verse shall stand Praising thy worth, despite his cruel hand.the same theme: no matter how great, good or beautiful you are, no day all these will fade away
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/sonnet60.md

item.title: The four steps (structure) of Shakespeare sonnet
item.description: Introduction (opening) Elucidation of the theme (developing; raising problems) Transition to another viewpoint (changing) Summing uo (concluding and may reinforce the theme; give a solution) 类似中国的 起承转合
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/The four steps (structure) of Shakespeare sonnet.md

item.title: sonnet 19
item.description: Devouring Time, blunt thou the lion’s paws,And make the earth devour her own sweet brood;Pluck the keen teeth from the fierce tiger’s jaws,And burn the long-liv’d phœnix in her blood; (lot's powerful and strong images to make readers feel the power of times)Make glad and sorry seasons as thou fleets, (glad and sorry season -> summer to winter --summer sonnet 18)And do whate’er thou wilt, swift-footed Time,To the wide world and all her fading sweets;But I forbid thee one most heinous crime: (heinous 十恶不赦的)O, carve not with thy hours my love’s fair brow,Nor draw no lines there with thine antique pen;Him in thy course untainted do allowFor beauty’s pattern to succeeding men. Yet do thy worst, old Time: despite thy wrong, My love shall in my verse ever live young.吞噬一切的时间啊，磨钝雄狮的利爪，教大地吞尽自己怀中的娇柔子嗣；从猛虎的颚间拔下它锋利的牙，令长寿的凤凰在血脉中燃尽生死；你飞驰而过，把四季翻作欢与悲，捷足的时间啊，这广阔寰宇之间，所有终将凋零的美好，你尽可施为，但我禁止你，犯下这桩最凶残的罪：哦，别用你的时辰在我爱郎的秀额刻划，别用你古旧的笔在那里描下皱纹千条；愿他在你的洪流里永远光洁无瑕，做后世众生仰望的美的范本与坐标。可你尽管使坏吧，老时间！任你万般摧残，我的爱人，将在我的诗行里，永远青春少年。
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/sonnet 19.md

item.title: Shakespeare's works publishcation
item.description: The first edition published by T.T(Thomas Torphe) may not get autherized not a good one size:Quarto second edition the worst edition have many changes by editor Some scholars revise the misttakes of second edition and become nowadays version 第一版出版致谢 Henry Wriothesley, Earl of Southampton William Herbert, Earl of Pembroke William Hathaway: "begetters", Shakespeare's wife
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/Shakespeare's works publishcation.md

item.title: Shakespeare's works' theme
item.description: Sonnet 1-126: to a fair youth(male) Sonnet 127-152:to a dark lady Sonnet 153,154:irrelevantPersuasion and procreation:(催婚和劝生) From fairest creatures we desire increase, That thereby beauty's rose might never die.(Sonnet 1. 1-2)Writing for eternity: But thy eternal summer shall not fade, Nor lose possession of that fair thou ow'st; Nor shall Death brag thou wander'st in his shade, When in eternal lines to time thou grow'st: So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see, So long lives this, and this gives life to thee. (Sonnet 18. 9-14)shame I may not evermore acknowledge thee, Lest my bewailed guilt should do thee shame; Nor thou with public kindness honor me, Unless thou take that honor from thy name.(sonnet 36. 9-12)Love triangle Ay me, but yet thou might'st my seat forbear, And chide thy beauty and thy straying youth, Who lead thee in their riot even there Where thou art forced to break a twofold truth: Hers, by thy beauty tempting her to thee, Thine, by thy beauty being false to me."(Sonnet 41.9-14)Departure How like a winter hath my absence been From thee, the pleasure of the fleeting year! What freezings have I felt, what dark days seen, Whato ld December's bareness everywhere!(Sonnet 97.1-4)Forgiveness: For to thy sensual fault I bring in sense- Thy adverse party is thy advocate- And 'gainst myself a lawful plea commence. Such civil war is in my love and hate That I an accessary needs must be To that sweet thief whi ch sourly robs from me.(Sonnet 35.11-14)For nothing this wide universe I call, Save thou, my rose; in it thou art my all.(Sonnet 109. 13-14)
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/Shakespeare's works' theme.md

item.title: sonnet55
item.description: Not marble, nor the gilded monuments Of princes, shall outlive this powerful rhyme; But you shall shine more bright in these contents Than unswept stone besmear'd with sluttish time. When wasteful war shall statues overturn, And broils root out the work of masonry, Nor Mars his sword nor war's quick fire shall burn The living record of your memory. Gainst death and all-oblivious enmity Shall you pace forth;your praise shall still find room Even in the eyes of all posterity That wear this world out to the ending doom. So, till the judgment that yourself arise, You live in this, and dwell in lover's eyes.
item.guid: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/sonnet55.md

item.title: William Shakespeare
item.description: Born in startford-upon-Avon,1564.4.26 learning in gramma school married early in his 18 | a tiger's heart warpt in player's(woman's原句) body published his first two long poems have his own theatre get a Coat of Arms(家徽)an experimental period 1591-1593 Titus Andronicus; Love's Labour's Lost; The Taming of Shrew; The Comdy Of Errors the second period 1594-1601 Romeo and Juliet; the Merchant of Venice; A Midsummer-Night's Dream; Much Ado about nothing; The Merry Wives Of Windsor; Juilius Ceaser the third period 1602-1610 Hamlet; Macbeth; Othello; King Lear; Twelfth Night; All's Well that Ends Well; Antony and Cleopatra the final period 1611 Cymbeline; Henry VIII; The Tempest
item.guid: My notes/English/莎士比亚文学赏析/subnotes/William Shakespeare.md
